text: Thank you for allowing us this opportunity to share our experience with robot assisted simple prostatectomy. Appreciation to Dr. Ross Anderson for his assistance in setting up this video. We generally perform this operation on patients with prostate glands greater than eighty centimeters cubed. Obviously we rule out prostate cancer and consider the general preoperative state of the patient.
An MRI can be helpful in assessing abnormal prostate anatomy or evaluating the size of the prostate. This particular prostate was greater than five hundred grams.

The patient is positioned in a steep, low-lithotomy position for the robot-assisted laparoscopic prostatectomy. The video shows a diagram and real image of the port placements, with insufflation occurring at the umbilicus.

The surgeon releases adhesions and drops the bladder, clearing perivascular fat. A transverse cystotomy is performed, revealing the ureteral orifices. Posterior dissection is initiated, extending laterally to identify the shoulders of the prostate and remove the adenoma.

The surgeon reflects the sigmoid colon and dissects the bladder to develop the space of Retzius. The medial umbilical ligament is grasped on the left side, and the dissection proceeds to the level of the vas deferens, visible in the center of the screen. A similar dissection is performed on the contralateral side.
The surgeon divides the urachus and drops the bladder, fully developing the space of Retzius, a step typical in robotic prostatectomy procedures.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar grasper in the left hand and monopolar scissors in the right hand to perform blunt dissection with bipolar hemostasis. A transverse cystotomy incision is made two to three centimeters posterior to the prostate adenoma, ensuring an adequate mucosal cuff of bladder tissue for closure.

The surgeon makes a long incision before entering the bladder, clearing off fat to aid in closure. Upon entering the bladder, the Foley catheter is visible, along with the prostate adenoma, which has a large intravascular medium lobe.

The surgeon creates a large cystotomy incision to fully expose the prostate, occasionally extending it during the mid portion of the dissection. Traction sutures are placed using a keef needle, which is passed through the exterior abdominal wall and the transverse cystotomy bladder neck cuff, typically using 2-0 or 0 proline sutures.
The surgeon applies traction to the prostate and bladder neck, providing adequate tension for easier dissection of the prostate adenoma. The tension can be adjusted as needed during the procedure.

The surgeon exposes the median lobe of the prostate and the ureteral orifices. A tenaculum is used to grasp the prostate, with caution to minimize bleeding. Large silk sutures may be used for retraction if a tenaculum is unavailable. Bladder stones are removed at this stage.

The surgeon places large bladder stones into a separate endocatch bag. A five French feeding catheter, cut to six inches and secured with a hemlock clip, is used to aid in identifying the ureteral orifices during the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon begins dissection posteriorly, carefully dissecting through the bladder neck until the surface of the adenoma is identified. Moderate bleeding is observed, and pinpoint cautery is used to control it while progressing through the procedure.

The surgeon reaches the shiny surface of the adenoma and uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to bluntly dissect the adenoma free from its pseudocapsule. The tenaculum provides traction during the dissection, revealing a few bladder neck fibers still attached to the adenoma.

The surgeon completes the posterior dissection and proceeds to dissect laterally and anteriorly around the prostate adenoma. The anterior tissue is typically minimal, but in some cases, there is a significant amount of anterior tissue compared to lateral lobe hyperplasia or median lobe tissue.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to divide the fused anterior commissure over the urethra. Blunt dissection combined with focal electrocautery is employed to separate bladder neck fibers from the prostate adenoma and control penetrating blood vessels.

The surgeon identifies the anatomy of the prostate adenoma, noting the curved 'shoulders' that extend downward towards the urethra. Using sharp dissection, the adenoma is separated from the prosthetic capsule, revealing the urethra with a catheter in place.

The surgeon selects an endocatch bag, typically ten millimeters, but adjusts to twelve or fifteen millimeters based on prostate size. Hemostasis is achieved using bipolar and monopolar electric cautery, along with figure-of-eight vicryl sutures. Bleeding is primarily controlled at the five and seven o'clock positions, near the prostate pedicles or urethral stump end.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis or retrigonization of the bladder using a 3-0 V-lock suture on a CB-23 tapered needle. The stitches are looped together to effectively retrigonize the bladder, sewing the trigone up to the three o'clock and nine o'clock positions on the urethral stump. The anterior urethra is tacked open to prevent bladder neck contractures.
The surgeon cinches the trigone of the bladder to the prosthetic stump by carefully pulling on each side of the suture, ensuring the connection is tension-free.

The surgeon addresses any persistent bleeding by suture ligating with a 3-0 Vicryl using an RV-1 needle in a figure-of-eight fashion. After completing the retrigonization of the bladder, a 22 French three-way Foley catheter is inserted.

The surgeon closes the cystotomy using a V-lock 2-0 suture with a GS-22 tapered needle, starting from the sides. Traction sutures are left in place to provide exposure of the bladder mucosa. After ensuring there are no leaks, a 19 round Blake drain is placed near the closure site.
A sample of adenoma tissue is displayed. The 22 French three-way Foley catheter is visibly left in place, and continuous bladder irrigation is applied until the urine appears light pink or clear.

There are several case series where people report their outcomes for robot-assisted simple prostatectomy. As you can see, their IPSS scores, as well as their Qmax, improved markedly following the operation. Patients note this improvement very quickly following surgery. Here are some of the complications reported in the literature. Several of these are very small studies. However, others are more in-depth. You can see there are relatively few complications. Obviously, this will vary depending on surgeon experience as well as the number of these particular cases that you've performed.

In terms of pointers for troubleshooting some complications, bleeding often resolves or improves when you remove the prostate adenoma. This also helps identify areas that need suture ligation. I find an air seal is helpful.

as one can suction more without losing the pneumoperitoneum. Occasionally, I will use hemostatic agents. Very rarely have I had to transfuse a patient, and I've never sent anyone to IR. In my experience, I've had one patient with a urine leak. This I think was a perforation through the prostate capsule. This eventually resolved with prolonged drainage. The patient did not require any further surgery.

I anticipate that one could experience a ureteral injury, although I've never had this myself. I've never had to reimplant a patient, but this could be done easily with a transparent robotic approach. The stents that I showed you earlier are helpful in identifying the ureter and making sure that you do not over-sew the ureteral orifice. Occasionally, patients can develop a bladder neck contraction. I find that endoscopic management works well for these patients. And one can simply incise the bladder neck contracture. Incontinence has not been an issue in my series, although this is possible and I discuss it with every patient. Thank you for allowing us the opportunity to share our experience with the robot-assisted simple prostatectomy.
